lessons.title: INTRODUCTION
topics:  Overview: Directions are instructions or guidelines that help individuals
navigate from one place to another or complete a specific task. They can be
given verbally, written down, or represented through symbols, maps, or
diagrams. Understanding and following directions is an essential skill in daily
life, as it ensures clarity, efficiency, and accuracy in movement and
activities: directions.
 Pair learners and introduce common phrases they can use when asking for directions.
Examples:
“Excuse me, how do I get to the bank?”
“Excuse” me, where is the nearest ATM or mobile money agent?”
"Excuse me, is there a market near here?”
 Explain that clear directions improve accessibility, prevent confusion, and save time,
especially in emergencies.
assessment: Project/Homework:
Assign a project-based task where learners create a simple map of their
town or community and indicate any key financial institutions, mobile
money agents, shops, and markets.
Learners to give an oral presentation on the map by describing how to
get to a bank, ATM, shop, or mobile money agent using proper

lessons.title: Expected Standard: Directions given appropriately
assessment:  Observe learners as they use appropriate language when asking for and
giving directions.
 Learners should interpret maps correctly and give directions accurately.
Summary.
 Reinforce key phrases for asking for directions.
 Emphasise key phrases for giving directions.

lessons.title: INTRODUCTION
topics:  Overview: Comprehension is an active process of understanding spoken or
written information. It is important for real-life tasks, like following
instructions, having conversations, and interpreting information. This topic
focuses on building basic comprehension skills, especially listening. Learners
will practice listening to passages, identifying main ideas, and joining
activities:  Explain to learners that listening requires concentration and the ability to filter main
ideas from supporting details.
 Read a story or informational passage.
 Tell learners to listen without writing.
 Tell learners to focus on the speaker, maintain silence, and mentally note key ideas.
Model Passage
“Kalulu and Suntwe: The Wisdom of the Calabash Bank”
Long ago, in a quiet village nestled near the great Zambezi River, lived

lessons.title: Where was the village in the story located?
What was Kalulu’s main weakness?
What natural disaster affected the village?
Why couldn’t Kalulu buy food during the rainy season?
What lesson did Kalulu learn from Suntwe by the end of the story?
In your opinion, why is it important to save money even when you have
topics: plenty?
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials:
 Model story
 Pre-recorded story
Expected Standard: Information listened to and comprehended correctly.
activities: Learners to fill in a table while the passage is being read.
Learners to answer comprehension questions based on the given
passage.
Summary:
 Comprehension is understanding spoken or written language.
 It is important for daily life and academic success.
 Listening comprehension involves paying attention, understanding, and
recalling spoken information.
assessment: Observe maximum participation in the activities.
Learners should demonstrate understanding of each learning activity
through the activities conducted.
Learners to fill in a table while the passage is being read.
Learners to answer comprehension questions based on the given
passage.

lessons.title: INTRODUCTION
topics:  Overview: Descriptive writing is an essential skill in both personal and professional
contexts, as it allows individuals to vividly convey experiences, emotions, and
observations to others. In real life, effective descriptive writing helps people
communicate more clearly and persuasively, whether it is in creative writing,
business reports, or everyday conversations. Descriptive writing plays a vital role in
activities:  Divide learners into groups and provide them with a descriptive passage to read.
Alternatively, read the description to the class.
The Banking System and the Importance of Saving Money
A bank is a place where people trust their money to be kept safe and
secure. As you walk inside, the cool, refreshing air greets you, and the
atmosphere is calm and organised. The floors gleam under the bright
fluorescent lights, reflecting the polished surfaces of the counters and
walls. The scent of freshly cleaned tiles fills the space, and the sound of

lessons.title: Expected Standard: Description of an object written correctly.
assessment:  Observe that every learner is able to identify descriptive words in texts.
 Learners to describe objects.
 Descriptive writing uses sensory details to create vivid mental images.
 Important features include structure (introduction, body, and conclusion) and
precise vocabulary.
 Practicing descriptive writing improves communication skills in everyday

lessons.title: INTRODUCTION
topics:  Overview: Contrast is an important concept in the English language that helps
express differences between ideas, events, or situations. In writing and speaking,
contrast allows for clearer communication by highlighting opposing thoughts.
Understanding contrast helps one improve their sentence structure, express their
thoughts more effectively, and engage in meaningful conversations. This topic
activities:  Introduce ‘but’ and ‘yet’ with examples and discuss their usage in sentences.
a. ‘But’ introduces an unexpected or opposite idea.
Example:
Mutinta is smart but she doesn’t spend her money wisely.”
b. ‘Yet’ is similar to ‘but’ but can imply a stronger contrast or a
surprising twist.
Example:
“He had all the money needed, yet he failed to buy school

lessons.title: Expected Standard: ‘but’ and ‘yet’ to show contrast in sentences used correctly.
activities: write them on the chart and display on the classroom wall.
 ‘But’ and ‘yet’ are used to show contrast between two ideas in a sentence.
 A comma is usually placed before ‘yet.’
 ‘Yet’ often carries a stronger sense of contrast than ‘but.’
 Identifying the main clause and the contrast clause helps in constructing correct
sentences.
 Effective use of contrast makes communication clearer and more engaging.
SUMMARY WRITING
assessment:  Learners construct sentences using ‘but’ and ‘yet’ correctly.
 Learners correctly punctuate sentences with contrast conjunctions.
 Learners explain why ‘but’ or ‘yet’ is used in a given sentence.
 Class Project: As an after-class activity, learners select ten best written sentences,
write them on the chart and display on the classroom wall.
 ‘But’ and ‘yet’ are used to show contrast between two ideas in a sentence.

lessons.title: INTRODUCTION
topics:  Overview: Short messages are a quick and effective way to communicate
important information in everyday life. Whether sending reminders, invitations,
or urgent updates, short messages must be clear and to the point. In this topic,
learners will appreciate the purpose of short messages and how to write them
effectively using simple, concise language. They will also learn to use
activities:  In pairs/groups, learners analyse different SMS samples displayed on a chart (or written
on the chalkboard) and identify their key features.
Examples:
i. LOL – Laugh out loud
ii. OMG - Oh my God
iii. BRB - Be Right Back
iv. TY - Thank You
v. IDK - I Don’t Know

lessons.title: Expected Standard: Short Message System in summary used correctly.
activities:  Put Learners into small groups and ask them to do the following:
a. Discuss proverbs
b. Interpret their meanings
c. State the situations in which proverbs are applied.
Example: Honesty is the best policy
Meaning: It is always best to tell the truth, even if it is difficult.
Application: Use it when advising someone struggling with telling the truth.
iv. Group representatives to present their work and teacher to give guidance during
assessment:  Learners compose clear and concise SMS messages.
 Learners use abbreviations and symbols appropriately.
 Learners differentiate between formal and informal SMS writing.
 Group Project/Homework: Learners to create a collection of SMS
messages for various real-life situations. These should be displayed on a
chart in the classroom. Learners should be encouraged to add new SMS

lessons.title: When offering advice or guidance to someone, proverbs can be used to convey a
topics: message in a concise and memorable way.
Example: Don’t count your chickens before they hatch.
Meaning: Don’t assume something will happen until it's confirmed.

lessons.title: Proverbs can be used to encourage and motivate someone to take action or
topics: persevere through challenges.
Example: When the going gets tough, the tough get going.
Meaning: When faced with difficulties, strong people will work harder to overcome

lessons.title: Proverbs can be used to warn someone of potential dangers or consequences.
topics: Example: Beware of the wolf in sheep’s clothing.
Meaning: Be cautious of people who appear friendly, but may have ulterior
motives.

lessons.title: Proverbs can be used to teach children and adults alike valuable life lessons and
topics: morals.
Example: Winners never quit and quitters never win.
Meaning: Success comes to those who persist and never give up, while those who
give up too easily never achieve victory.

lessons.title: Proverbs can be used to offer comfort and consolation to someone who is
topics: experiencing difficulties or hardships.
Example: Time heals all wounds.
Meaning: With time, the pain and sadness will lessen.

lessons.title: Proverbs can be used to celebrate someone’s successes or achievements.
topics: Example: The fruit of your labour is sweet.
Meaning: The rewards of your hard work are enjoyable and satisfying.

lessons.title: Proverbs can be used to describe a situation or circumstance in a vivid and
topics: memorable way.
Example: It’s raining cats and dogs.
Meaning: It’s raining very heavily.

lessons.title: Proverbs can be used to add humour or wit to a conversation or situation.
topics: Example: “Don’t cry over spilled milk.”
Meaning: Don't waste time feeling sorry for something that's already happened and
can’t be changed.
activities:  Group learners and ask them to interpret given proverbs:

lessons.title: A bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.
topics: Meaning: It is better to appreciate what you already have than to take a risk to get
something more.
Interpretation: This proverb encourages people to be content with what they have and

lessons.title: Actions speak louder than words.
topics: Meaning: What you do is more important than what you say.
Interpretation: This proverb highlights the importance of taking action and following
through on your commitments, rather than just talking about them.

lessons.title: All’s fair in love and war.
topics: Meaning: In situations where emotions are high or the stakes are great, normal rules and
conventions may not apply.
Interpretation: This proverb suggests that in extreme circumstances, people may do
things they wouldn’t normally do, and that’s just the way it is.

lessons.title: An apple a day keeps the doctor away.
topics: Meaning: Eating healthy foods can help prevent illnesses.
Interpretation: This proverb encourages people to take care of their physical health by
making healthy choices, like eating nutritious food.

lessons.title: Blood is thicker than water.
topics: Meaning: Family ties are stronger than any other relationship.
Interpretation: This proverb highlights the importance of family bonds and suggests that
they should take priority over other relationships.

lessons.title: Give someone an inch and they’ll take a mile.
topics: Meaning: If you give someone a small amount of freedom or power, they’ll likely take
advantage of it and try to take more.
Interpretation: This proverb warns people against being too generous or permissive, as
it can lead to exploitation.

lessons.title: Practice what you preach.
topics: Meaning: You should do what you advise others to do.
Interpretation: This proverb encourages people not to be hypocritical and to live by the
principles they claim to believe in.

lessons.title: Rome was not built in a day.
topics: Meaning: Big things take time to accomplish.
Interpretation: This proverb encourages people to be patient and to recognise that
achieving great things requires time, effort, and perseverance.

lessons.title: The grass is always greener on the other side.
topics: Meaning: People often think someone else’s situation is better than their own, but it is
not always true.
Interpretation: This proverb warns people against comparing themselves to others and
encourages them to appreciate what they have.
“When in Rome, do as the Romans do.”
activities:  Ask learners to research on proverbs used in the local community, providing
interpretations and situations when to use them.
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials:
 Worksheets/Texts with proverbs
 Audios

lessons.title: Expected Standard: Proverbs in various social contexts used appropriately.
activities: information or texts.
 Ask learners to Skim through the passage to get the general idea
 Ask learners to read the passage
 Time learners to ensure that they read at a speed of 200 words per minute.
 Guide learners to avoid bad reading habits
assessment:  Pair learners and provide them with proverbs to interpret.
 Give learners situations and ask them to supply proverbs that could be used.
 Proverbs are well known sayings that offer guidance or advice.
 They also declare a well-acknowledged fact or universal truth.
 Proverbs are often metaphorical.
 They are a collection of generations of wisdom and applies to life on Earth.

lessons.title: Inclusive education is an approach that values diversity and promotes
topics: the participation of all learners, regardless of their abilities or
disabilities. It involves creating a learning environment that is
welcoming, supportive, and challenging for all learners. Inclusive
education recognises that every learner has unique needs, interests, and
learning styles, and seeks to provide opportunities for all learners to

lessons.title: One of the key principles of inclusive education is the concept of
topics: Universal Design for Learning (UDL). UDL involves designing learning
experiences that are accessible and engaging for all learners, regardless
of their abilities or disabilities. This can involve using multimedia
materials, providing choices and options, and incorporating assistive
technology.

lessons.title: Inclusive education also requires teachers to be flexible and adaptable in
topics: their teaching practices. This can involve differentiating instruction to
meet the diverse needs of learners, using assistive technology to support
learners with disabilities, and providing opportunities for learners to
take ownership of their learning.
Teaching Points:
activities: Abilities – skills or talents.
Environment – the surrounding conditions where learning takes place.
Supportive – giving help or encouragement.
Challenging – difficult in a way that tests ability.
Unique – one of a kind; different from others.
Learning styles – different ways people learn best.
Potential – the ability to develop or succeed.
Universal Design for Learning (UDL) – an educational framework to improve

lessons.title: What is the main goal of inclusive education?
topics: A. To separate learners with disabilities from other students
B. To provide opportunities for all learners to reach their full potential
C. To focus only on academic achievement
D. To ignore the diverse needs of learners

lessons.title: What is Universal Design for Learning (UDL)?
topics: A. A teaching method that focuses only on learners with disabilities
B. A learning environment that is welcoming and supportive for all learners
C. A design approach that involves creating learning experiences accessible and
engaging for all learners
D. A technology that helps learners with disabilities
activities: B. A learning environment that is welcoming and supportive for all learners
C. A design approach that involves creating learning experiences accessible and
engaging for all learners
D. A technology that helps learners with disabilities

lessons.title: What are some ways that teachers can create a welcoming and supportive learning
topics: environment for learners with diverse needs?

lessons.title: How can assistive technology be used to support learners with disabilities in the
topics: classroom?
